headline: Black Tuesday and the Great Depression's Impact on Montana, 1929
body:
The ticker tape in New York stopped chattering on October 29, 1929, but the blood had been draining from Montana for a decade. The men in the tailored suits called it a crash. The men in the fields called it the final turn of the screw. They had been bled dry by the banks, by the railroads, by the sky itself. Wheat that brought two dollars a bushel in 1918 fell to ninety cents in 1928. By 1932, it would fetch thirty cents. You cannot feed a family on thirty-cent wheat. You cannot pay the mortgage on thirty-cent wheat. You can only watch the dust bury the fence posts and wait for the sheriff to arrive.

In Butte, the Anaconda Copper Mining Company watched the price of copper plummet. Their response was the response of capital everywhere: they cut the men. Thousands of miners, men who had coughed up their lungs in the dark to build the company's wealth, were thrown into the street. The smelters in Anaconda went cold. The trains stopped rolling. The company towns became ghost towns, populated by hollow-eyed men standing in breadlines, their pockets empty, their hands idle. By 1932, one out of every four men, women, and children in the state of Montana was surviving on government relief. 

The desperation was not a statistic; it was a living, breathing agony. It was written in the letters that flooded the desk of Governor John E. Erickson. On August 2, 1930, Mrs. H. Fredrickson sat down in Opheim, Montana, and took up her pen. She did not write of abstract economic theories. She wrote of the brutal arithmetic of survival. "I would like your advice on what to do," she pleaded. "My Husband and I came to Montana in 1913 to make our home here and have worked very hard to try to make a home but it seems as if we can not raise means enough to do so any more 4 years ago my husband had a accident and lost the use of his right hand so he can not use it any more, so that leaves all the heavy farm work for the rest of us." 

This was the reality of the disaster. It was not a line on a graph. It was a one-armed man watching his wife break her back in the dust of Opheim. It was the foreclosure notices nailed to the doors of homesteads from the Dakotas to the Divide. The men who gambled on margin in Wall Street lost their paper fortunes, but the men and women of Montana lost the earth beneath their feet. They were the collateral damage of a system built on greed, left to starve in the richest nation on earth.
